section.title: The Last Supper
section.sectionRationale: As Passover nears and Satan enters Judas, Jesus gathers His apostles for a meal He has longed to share. He takes bread and cup and gives them a new meaning: this is My body given for you, this cup is the new covenant in My blood poured out for you. At the very table where He gives Himself, a betrayer's hand rests and a quarrel over greatness breaks out, so He redefines greatness as service and promises His own a kingdom. He foretells Peter's denial and His own numbering with transgressors, showing that His death is the fulfillment of Scripture and the ground of a new covenant.
section.thesis: At the Passover table Jesus reveals His death as the sacrifice that seals a new covenant, His body given and His blood poured out for His people, and He calls those who share His meal to greatness through service and to perseverance through His intercession.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the plot and Judas's betrayal; the preparation and institution of the Supper; the dispute over greatness and the promise of the kingdom; and the prediction of Peter's denial with the word about the two swords.

verses.text: Now the Feast of Unleavened Bread, called the Passover, was approaching,
and the chief priests and scribes were looking for a way to put Jesus to death, for they feared the people.
Then Satan entered Judas Iscariot, who was one of the Twelve.
And Judas went to discuss with the chief priests and temple officers how he might betray Jesus to them.
They were delighted and agreed to give him money.
Judas consented, and began to look for an opportunity to betray Jesus to them in the absence of a crowd.

units.label: The institution of the Supper
units.note: this is My body, given for you

verses.text: Then came the day of Unleavened Bread on which the Passover lamb was to be sacrificed.
Jesus sent Peter and John, saying, “Go and make preparations for us to eat the Passover.”
“Where do You want us to prepare it?” they asked.
He answered, “When you enter the city, a man carrying a jug of water will meet you. Follow him to the house he enters,
and say to the owner of that house, ‘The Teacher asks: Where is the guest room, where I may eat the Passover with My disciples?’
And he will show you a large upper room, already furnished. Make preparations there.”
So they went and found it just as Jesus had told them. And they prepared the Passover.
When the hour had come, Jesus reclined at the table with His apostles.
And He said to them, “I have eagerly desired to eat this {{pascha|Passover}} with you before My suffering.
For I tell you that I will not eat it again until it is fulfilled in the kingdom of God.”
After taking the cup, He gave thanks and said, “Take this and divide it among yourselves.
For I tell you that I will not drink of the fruit of the vine from now on until the kingdom of God comes.”
And He took the bread, gave thanks and broke it, and gave it to them, saying, “This is My body, given for you; do this in {{anamnesis|remembrance}} of Me.”
In the same way, after supper He took the cup, saying, “This cup is the new {{diatheke|covenant}} in My blood, which is poured out for you.
Look! The hand of My betrayer is with Mine on the table.
Indeed, the Son of Man will go as it has been determined, but woe to that man who betrays Him.”
Then they began to question among themselves which of them was going to do this.

units.label: Greatness through service and the promised kingdom
units.note: I am among you as one who serves

verses.text: A dispute also arose among the disciples as to which of them should be considered the greatest.
So Jesus declared, “The kings of the Gentiles lord it over them, and those in authority over them call themselves benefactors.
But you shall not be like them. Instead, the greatest among you should be like the youngest, and the one who leads like the one who serves.
For who is greater, the one who reclines at the table or the one who serves? Is it not the one who reclines? But I am among you as one who {{diakoneo|serves}}.
You are the ones who have stood by Me in My trials.
And I bestow on you a kingdom, just as My Father has bestowed one on Me,
so that you may eat and drink at My table in My kingdom and sit on thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel.

units.label: Peter's denial foretold and the two swords
units.note: I have prayed for you, that your faith will not fail

verses.text: Simon, Simon, Satan has asked to sift all of you like wheat.
But I have prayed for you, Simon, that your faith will not fail. And when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers.”
“Lord,” said Peter, “I am ready to go with You even to prison and to death.”
But Jesus replied, “I tell you, Peter, the rooster will not crow today until you have denied three times that you know Me.”
Then Jesus asked them, “When I sent you out without purse or bag or sandals, did you lack anything?” “Nothing,” they answered.
“Now, however,” He told them, “the one with a purse should take it, and likewise a bag; and the one without a sword should sell his cloak and buy one.
For I tell you that this Scripture must be fulfilled in Me: ‘And He was numbered with the transgressors.’ For what is written about Me is reaching its fulfillment.”
So they said, “Look, Lord, here are two swords.” “That is enough,” He answered.

pascha.gloss: Passover, the feast recalling Israel's rescue from Egypt by the blood of the lamb. Jesus eagerly desires to eat this Passover, and then transforms it: He is the true Passover Lamb whose blood delivers His people, and the meal now points forward to its fulfillment in the kingdom of God.

anamnesis.gloss: Remembrance, a calling to mind that makes the past present. Do this in remembrance of Me is more than nostalgia; the Supper proclaims and applies afresh the once-for-all death of Christ, so that His people continually receive its benefits and look ahead to His return.

diatheke.gloss: Covenant, a binding arrangement God establishes. Jesus names the cup the new covenant in His blood, fulfilling Jeremiah's promise of a covenant of forgiven sins and changed hearts. The new covenant is sealed not with the blood of animals but with the poured-out blood of the Son.

diakoneo.gloss: To serve, to wait on others, to minister. Against the disciples' quarrel over greatness, Jesus points to Himself: I am among you as one who serves. True greatness in His kingdom is measured by lowly service, the pattern of the Lord who serves His people to the point of laying down His life.

basic.context: Passover approaches, and the leaders look for a way to kill Jesus. Satan enters Judas, who agrees to betray Him for money. Jesus sends Peter and John to prepare the meal, and gathers with His apostles, saying He has eagerly longed to eat this Passover before He suffers. He takes bread and cup and gives them new meaning: this is My body, given for you; this cup is the new covenant in My blood, poured out for you. Even as He gives Himself, He points out that the betrayer's hand is on the table. When the disciples argue over who is greatest, He teaches that greatness is service, for He Himself is among them as one who serves, and He promises them a kingdom and thrones. He warns Peter that Satan will sift him, but that He has prayed for him, and foretells his threefold denial. Finally He speaks of buying a sword, showing that He is about to be numbered with transgressors.
basic.hermeneutics: The Supper is the interpretive key: Jesus explains His approaching death as a Passover sacrifice that seals a new covenant, His body and blood given for His people. Around this center Luke places the betrayal, the dispute over greatness, and Peter's denial, all showing that the new covenant community is formed not by human strength but by the self-giving of Christ and His intercession.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus longs to eat the pascha, the Passover, and transforms it around Himself. He commands the Supper as an anamnesis, a remembrance that makes His death ever-present to faith. The cup is the new diatheke, covenant, in His blood. And He defines greatness by pointing to Himself as the one who diakoneo, serves.
basic.typology: The Passover lamb whose blood spared Israel points to Christ, the true Lamb given for His people. The new covenant fulfills Jeremiah's promise of forgiven sins. The numbering with transgressors fulfills Isaiah's suffering servant, who was counted among sinners and bore their sin.
basic.christ: Jesus is the true Passover Lamb, the host who gives His own body and blood, and the servant King who is among His people as one who serves. He is the suffering servant numbered with transgressors, and the risen Lord who prays for Peter and bestows a kingdom on His own.
basic.god: God's saving purpose stands sovereign even over betrayal, for the Son of Man goes as it has been determined. God establishes a new covenant of forgiveness in the blood of His Son, and He bestows a kingdom on the lowly who serve. His grace upholds failing disciples through the intercession of Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 12 institutes the Passover with the lamb's blood. Jeremiah 31 promises the new covenant of forgiven sins. Isaiah 53 foretells the servant numbered with transgressors. 1 Corinthians 11 hands on the words of institution to the church.
basic.oneStory: At the Passover table the whole story of redemption gathers to a point: the true Lamb gives Himself to seal a new covenant of forgiveness. The Supper looks back to Egypt and forward to the kingdom feast, and it forms a people who share Christ's meal, follow His path of service, and are kept by His prayer until they eat and drink at His table in His kingdom.

context.literary: Luke sets the Last Supper at the head of the passion narrative, framing Jesus' death with His own interpretation of it. Distinctive to Luke are the two cups, the dispute over greatness placed at the table, and the tender word to Peter about intercession. The scene binds the Supper, the betrayal, and the coming denials into one movement about the founding of the new covenant people.
context.historical: The Passover recalled the exodus and was eaten with lamb, unleavened bread, and cups of wine, each with covenantal meaning. Betrayal for money and the arranging of a secret handover fit the leaders' fear of the crowd. Reclining at table was the posture of a festal meal. To be numbered with transgressors evoked the shameful death of a criminal, which Jesus embraces as the fulfillment of Scripture.
context.canonical: The words of institution are among the most important in Scripture, handed on by Paul and shaping Christian worship ever since. They gather the Passover of Exodus, the covenant blood of Sinai, the new covenant of Jeremiah, and the suffering servant of Isaiah into the person of Jesus. The Supper anticipates the marriage supper of the Lamb in Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present Jesus' own interpretation of His death as a Passover sacrifice sealing a new covenant, His body and blood given for His people, and to form a covenant community marked by service rather than status and sustained by Christ's intercession rather than its own strength.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the founding events, but the command 'do this in remembrance of Me' is directly prescriptive, instituting the ongoing Supper. The teaching on greatness through service is a binding pattern for Jesus' people, while the betrayal and denial are described as warnings, not models.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean by 'This is My body' and 'This cup is the new covenant in My blood'?

views.label: A covenant sign that truly conveys His self-giving death
views.case: The bread and cup are given their meaning by Jesus' words: His body given and His blood poured out for His people, sealing the new covenant of Jeremiah. The Supper is a covenant meal that proclaims and applies His atoning death, so that those who receive it in faith share its benefits.

views.label: A memorial that proclaims His death until He comes
views.case: The stress on 'in remembrance of Me' and the forward look to the kingdom frame the Supper as a proclaiming remembrance, a covenant sign pointing to the once-for-all sacrifice rather than repeating it. It unites the church with Christ's death by faith as it awaits His return.

debates.bearing: Christians differ on how Christ is present, but agree that the Supper is the covenant meal of His people, proclaiming His body given and His blood shed for the forgiveness of sins and binding them to Him until He comes.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean about buying a sword?

views.label: A vivid warning of coming hostility, not a call to arms
views.case: Jesus links the sword to being 'numbered with transgressors' and, when they produce two swords, says 'that is enough', then rebukes their actual use of the sword at His arrest. The saying dramatizes the danger and rejection about to fall on Him and His followers, not a literal command to fight.

views.label: A real, if limited, provision for self-defense in a hostile world
views.case: Some take the instruction more literally as acknowledging that the disciples enter a season of danger and hardship, contrasting the earlier peaceful mission. Yet even so it is bounded, for Jesus heals the wounded servant and forbids further violence.

debates.bearing: Either way Jesus is not authorizing the cause of the kingdom to advance by the sword; He heals His captor's servant and goes willingly to fulfill the Scripture, showing His path is suffering, not force.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus says He has eagerly desired to eat this pascha, the Passover, and then reshapes the ancient feast around Himself, for He is the true Lamb whose death delivers His people. He commands the meal to be kept as an anamnesis, a remembrance that does not merely recall a distant event but makes His once-for-all death present and effective to faith. The cup He names the new diatheke, the covenant promised by Jeremiah, sealed not in the blood of animals but in His own blood poured out for the forgiveness of sins. And when the disciples grasp for greatness, He overturns the world's scale by pointing to Himself as the one who diakoneo, who serves, the Lord who waits on His people and will lay down His life for them. Around this table the vocabulary of Passover, covenant, remembrance, and service defines a new people.

typology.type: The Passover lamb whose blood spared Israel, Exodus 12
typology.fulfillment: Christ the true Passover Lamb, His body given and blood poured out for His people, Luke 22:15-20.

typology.type: The new covenant of forgiven sins, Jeremiah 31:31-34
typology.fulfillment: The cup as the new covenant in Jesus' blood, Luke 22:20.

typology.type: The servant numbered with transgressors, Isaiah 53:12
typology.fulfillment: Jesus reckoned among criminals in His arrest and death, Luke 22:37.

study.christ: Jesus is at once the host and the sacrifice of this meal: the true Passover Lamb whose body is given and whose blood is poured out to seal a new covenant of forgiveness. He is the servant King who, at the very hour His disciples squabble over rank, kneels in spirit among them as one who serves, and who will serve them to death. He is the suffering servant of Isaiah, willingly numbered with transgressors that His people might be counted righteous. And He is the risen and reigning Lord in advance, who prays for Peter so that his faith will not fail and who bestows on His own a kingdom and a place at His table. Every thread of the meal is woven from His self-giving love.
study.god: The God of the Last Supper is sovereign over the darkest treachery, for the Son of Man goes as it has been determined even as woe falls on the betrayer. He is the covenant God who now establishes, in the blood of His Son, the new covenant of forgiven sins and transformed hearts long promised through the prophets. He bestows a kingdom on lowly servants rather than on the self-promoting, overturning the world's scale of greatness. And He upholds weak and failing disciples not by their resolve but by the intercession of Christ, so that even Peter, sifted by Satan, will turn back and strengthen others. This is a God who saves by self-giving love and keeps His people by grace.

crossReferences.note: The Passover lamb and its blood that spared Israel, fulfilled in Christ the true Lamb.
The promise of a new covenant of forgiven sins, sealed in the cup of Jesus' blood.
He was numbered with the transgressors, the Scripture Jesus says must be fulfilled in Him.
Paul hands on the words of institution, showing the Supper proclaims the Lord's death until He comes.

study.oneStory: At the Passover table the whole story of redemption comes to its point: the true Lamb gives His body and blood to seal a new covenant of forgiveness. The Supper looks back to the exodus and forward to the kingdom feast, and around it Jesus forms a people marked not by grasping for greatness but by service, kept not by their own strength but by His prayer. As the betrayer slips away and the denials loom, the King goes willingly to be numbered with transgressors, so that those who share His meal may one day eat and drink at His table in His kingdom.

quiz.q: What new meaning does Jesus give to the bread and the cup?

options.text: His body given for them and the cup as the new covenant in His blood, poured out for them.
options.note: Luke 22:19-20 gives the body given and the new covenant in His blood.

options.text: A remembrance of His coming death for His disciples.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific new covenant sealed in His poured-out blood.

options.text: A continuation of the old Passover unchanged.
options.note: He transforms the Passover around Himself, instituting something new.

options.text: A symbol with no connection to His death.
options.note: The bread and cup point directly to His body given and blood poured out.

quiz.q: How does Jesus redefine greatness when the disciples argue about it?

options.text: The greatest should be like the youngest and the leader like one who serves, as He is among them as one who serves.
options.note: Luke 22:26-27 grounds greatness in service, modeled by Jesus Himself.

options.text: The greatest among them must serve the others.
options.note: True, but it omits that Jesus points to Himself as the serving one who defines the pattern.

options.text: Greatness comes from having authority over others.
options.note: That is the way of the Gentile kings, which Jesus tells them not to imitate.

options.text: They should stop caring about the kingdom altogether.
options.note: Jesus bestows a kingdom on them; He redirects greatness, not the kingdom.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say to Simon Peter about his coming trial?

options.text: That Satan asked to sift him, but Jesus has prayed that his faith will not fail, and he must later strengthen his brothers.
options.note: Luke 22:31-32 shows Satan's request, Jesus' intercession, and the call to strengthen others.

options.text: That He has prayed for Peter so his faith will not fail.
options.note: True and central, but it omits Satan's sifting and the call to strengthen the brothers afterward.

options.text: That Peter is strong enough to withstand the coming test.
options.note: Jesus foretells Peter's denial; his keeping rests on Christ's prayer, not his own strength.

options.text: That Peter will never be shaken at all.
options.note: Jesus foretells that Peter will deny Him three times.

quiz.q: What does Jesus reveal about His death when He speaks of the two swords and Scripture?

options.text: That He must be numbered with the transgressors, fulfilling what is written about Him.
options.note: Luke 22:37 cites Isaiah 53:12: He was numbered with transgressors.

options.text: That difficult and dangerous days were coming for His followers.
options.note: True of the context, but the stated point is the fulfillment of Scripture in His being counted a criminal.

options.text: That the disciples should arm themselves to defend the kingdom.
options.note: He rejects the use of the sword at His arrest; the saying dramatizes rejection, not armed defense.

options.text: That He would escape His enemies by force.
options.note: He goes willingly to fulfill Scripture, not to fight His way free.

quiz.q: How does Luke show that God's purpose stands even over the betrayal?

options.text: Jesus says the Son of Man goes as it has been determined, even as woe falls on the betrayer.
options.note: Luke 22:22 holds together divine determination and human responsibility.

options.text: Jesus knows in advance that Judas will betray Him.
options.note: True, but it omits that His going is according to God's determined plan, not mere foreknowledge.

options.text: God forces Judas to betray Jesus against his will.
options.note: Judas acts willingly and is held guilty; God's purpose stands without excusing him.

options.text: The betrayal takes God by surprise and derails His plan.
options.note: The opposite: the Son of Man goes exactly as it was determined.

questions: Jesus said He eagerly desired to share this meal before He suffered. How does it move you to know that His heart was set on giving Himself for you?
The Supper is a remembrance that proclaims His body given and blood poured out. When you take the bread and cup, are you receiving them as more than ritual, as Christ's own self-giving for you?
At the table where Jesus gave Himself, the disciples argued about who was greatest. Where does the hunger for status still creep into your following of a Lord who serves?
Jesus told Peter, I have prayed for you, that your faith will not fail. How does it steady you to know that your perseverance rests on Christ's intercession and not on your own strength?
When Peter is restored he is to strengthen his brothers. How might God want to use your own failures and restorations to strengthen others?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts end verse 19 after 'This is My body' and omit the words that follow, including verse 20. The BSB includes the longer, well-attested text with the second cup and the new covenant saying, which agrees with Paul's account in 1 Corinthians 11.
Jesus quotes Isaiah 53:12, 'And He was numbered with the transgressors', applying the suffering servant prophecy directly to Himself as He goes to be arrested and executed among criminals.

The instruction to buy a sword, followed by 'That is enough', is best read as a vivid warning of coming hostility rather than a call to arms, since Jesus heals the servant wounded at His arrest and forbids further violence.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 22:1-38. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
